positions.name: Closed Guard
positions.description: Guard player has legs locked around opponent's waist. One of the most fundamental positions in BJJ.

techniques.name: Scissor Sweep
techniques.goal: Sweep opponent from closed guard to mount by creating a scissoring motion with your legs — one leg pushes across their belly while the other cuts their base away.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back, guard OPEN, torso angled to the side of your collar grip. Not flat on your back — hips are scooted slightly to the collar grip side.
initialPosition.grips: Deep cross collar grip (4 fingers inside, same side you're sweeping toward) + sleeve or wrist grip on their opposite arm, pulling it across.
initialPosition.legs: Top leg: shin placed horizontally across opponent's stomach/chest, knee pointing to the ceiling. Bottom leg: extended along the mat, foot hooking behind opponent's far knee/calf.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the collar grip side, slightly off-center. Never flat on your back.

greenLights: Opponent is kneeling with upright posture (not standing)
Opponent's weight is centered or slightly forward
You've broken their posture and they're recovering upright — transitional moment
Opponent's base is narrow (knees close together)
redLights: Opponent is standing up — your shin shield won't be effective
Opponent has a very wide base with knees spread far apart
Opponent has underhooks on both sides
You don't have a solid collar grip yet (shallow grip = no pull power)
steps: From closed guard, secure deep cross collar grip and opposite-side sleeve grip. Open your guard.
Place your shin across opponent's belly, angle your hips toward the collar grip side. Extend your bottom leg out, foot hooking behind their far knee.
Simultaneously: PULL forward and down with the collar grip, PUSH with the shin across their belly, and SWEEP the bottom leg back to cut away their far knee/base. All three happen together.
Follow the sweep momentum to mount, keeping the collar grip throughout the transition. Establish mount control immediately.
complexpieces: If opponent posts their hand to resist the sweep, switch to an armbar on the posted arm
Chain with triangle: if they posture to resist and leave an arm in/arm out situation
Lapel lasso variation: feed the lapel under their leg for a much stronger sweep grip
simplifications: Practice the scissoring leg motion without grips first — focus on body mechanics
Use double sleeve grips instead of collar grip for an easier grip setup
Against a cooperative partner, focus only on the timing of the hip angle change

techniques.name: Hip Bump Sweep
techniques.goal: Sweep opponent to mount by explosively sitting up and driving your hips into them when they posture up. Capitalizes on their upright posture.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from lying on back to sitting up explosively. Posting on one hand behind you on the mat.
initialPosition.grips: Posting hand flat on the mat behind you (same side as sweep direction). Free hand reaches over opponent's same-side shoulder in an overhook/hugging position.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Foot on the posting-hand side plants flat on the mat for base and driving power.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and upward at approximately 45° angle toward the overhook side.

greenLights: Opponent sits upright with hands on your chest, hips, or completely off you
Opponent has no collar or sleeve grips at the moment
Opponent leans back or postures up — creating space between your chests
Opponent momentarily takes both hands off you
redLights: Opponent has heavy forward pressure with head low on your chest
Opponent has deep collar grips controlling your posture down
Opponent is already actively working a pass
You're flat on the mat with no explosiveness available in your hips
steps: The MOMENT opponent postures up, open your guard and plant your foot (same side as posting hand) flat on the mat.
Post your hand on the mat behind you and EXPLOSIVELY sit up, driving your hips forward and UP at a 45° angle.
Reach over their shoulder on the same side with your free arm, hugging tight to prevent them from posting their hand.
Drive through to mount, pushing them onto their back with the hip bump momentum. Secure mount control immediately.
complexpieces: If opponent posts their hand to block → grab the posted wrist for a kimura
If opponent pushes your chest → dive under their arm for guillotine
Combine with scissor sweep: attempt hip bump (they lean back), when they push forward, immediately transition to scissor sweep
simplifications: Practice the explosive sit-up motion without the sweep (just drill sitting up fast from guard)
Focus on the hip driving angle (45°) against a static partner
Do the sweep without the overhook first — just the sit-up and hip drive

techniques.name: Armbar from Closed Guard
techniques.goal: Hyperextend opponent's elbow joint by pivoting your hips perpendicular to their body, clamping their arm between your legs, and lifting your hips against the elbow.

initialPosition.body: Lying on back in closed guard. Hips already angled toward the attacking side — perpendicular angle to opponent's body is the target.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side wrist or sleeve grip on the arm you're attacking. Other hand grips their head (behind the neck) or collar, pulling their posture down.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens to place one foot on their hip (same side as trapped arm) for the pivot. Other leg will come high across their back and over their face.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted to the side, creating a perpendicular angle. Never square/flat.

greenLights: Opponent extends one arm — posting on your chest, reaching for your collar, or pushing
Opponent's posture is broken down (head low, leaning forward)
You've successfully isolated one of their arms with wrist control
Opponent is focused on posturing up and not defending their arm
redLights: Both opponent's arms are tight to their body — nothing to isolate
Opponent is actively posturing up with hips back and strong base
You're flat on your back with no angle created yet
Opponent is stacking you (hips driving over your head)
steps: Control the target arm at the wrist. Use your other hand to grab behind their head or collar and PULL their posture down.
Place your foot on their hip (same side as trapped arm) and pivot your hips to create a perpendicular angle to their body. Your other leg swings high across their back and over their shoulders/face.
Clamp BOTH legs tight — one leg over their face/neck, one across their torso. Keep their thumb pointing UP (arm fully extended, elbow joint facing your hips).
Squeeze knees together, pinch tight. Lift your hips UP against their elbow for the submission. Control at the wrist — your hips provide the breaking pressure.
complexpieces: Chain from triangle: attempt triangle first, if they pull arm out, switch to armbar
If they stack you, transition to triangle or omoplata
Set up with a cross collar choke attempt — when they push your hand away, catch the extended arm
simplifications: Drill the hip pivot/angle change in isolation (just movement, no submission)
Break into 2 parts: (1) angle creation + leg placement, (2) finishing mechanics
Practice leg clamp and hip lift on a compliant partner's arm to feel correct pressure

techniques.name: Triangle Choke
techniques.goal: Choke opponent using your legs in a figure-four configuration around their neck and one trapped arm. The legs compress the carotid arteries using the thigh and their own shoulder.

initialPosition.body: Lying on back in closed guard. Hips angled to the side for leg clearance over their shoulder.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side sleeve grip on the arm you're pulling across your body. Other hand on their collar or behind their head, pushing them down.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. One leg will throw HIGH over their shoulder and behind their neck. Other foot pushes on their hip for the pivot.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted out to the side to create the angle needed for your leg to clear over their shoulder.

greenLights: Opponent has one arm forward and one arm back (arm in, arm out situation)
Opponent pushes one hand into your chest or stomach — arm is extended and exposed
You've broken their posture and can control one arm across your centerline
After a failed guard pass attempt where one arm is isolated
redLights: Both of opponent's arms are inside your guard — can't create arm in/arm out
Opponent has strong upright posture with hips back (can't throw leg over)
Opponent is stacking you (hips driving over your head)
You have no grip on any of their arms — can't control the arm-in position
steps: Control one arm by the wrist/sleeve and PULL it across your centerline. Push their head down with your other hand.
Open guard, plant your foot on their hip (opposite side of trapped arm), and throw your top leg HIGH over their shoulder and behind their neck.
Lock the triangle: place the back of your knee over your opposite ankle (figure-four). Angle your body 30° to the side to tighten the choke geometry.
Squeeze knees together, pull their head DOWN with both hands, lift your hips. Their trapped arm's shoulder compresses one carotid, your thigh compresses the other.
complexpieces: If they posture inside the triangle, switch to armbar on the trapped arm
If they try to stack, release the lock and transition to omoplata by controlling the shoulder
Set up from overhook: swim for an overhook control, then throw the triangle leg over their shoulder
simplifications: Practice the figure-four leg lock configuration without any resistance (just position your legs correctly)
Focus first on getting the arm-in, arm-out position before throwing the leg
Wall drills: lie on your back with hips near a wall and practice the hip-cut angle

techniques.name: Cross Collar Choke
techniques.goal: Choke opponent using both hands gripping deep inside their collar in an X pattern. Wrist bones compress the carotid arteries. Gi-only technique.

initialPosition.body: Lying on back in closed guard. Opponent's posture is broken down — ideally chest to chest.
initialPosition.grips: First hand: deep 4-finger-inside grip in the cross collar (palm up, reaching across). Second hand: deep grip inside the OPPOSITE collar (palm down). Wrists form an X at the throat.
initialPosition.legs: Guard closed tight, actively pulling opponent's posture down with leg squeeze.
initialPosition.hips: Can be square or slightly angled. Focus is on keeping opponent's posture broken so they can't escape.

greenLights: Opponent's posture is fully broken down and their head is low
You've secured the first collar grip very deep (past the tag at the back of the neck)
Opponent is focused on passing guard and not defending their collar
Opponent is flat-chested on you, unable to posture up
redLights: Opponent has strong posture with hips back — can't reach collar deeply
Opponent is actively grip-fighting and stripping your collar grips
You can't get deeper than halfway in the collar (too shallow = no choke)
Opponent stands up in your guard (lose the broken posture)
steps: Break opponent's posture: pull with legs and arms. Secure first grip — reach across and feed your hand DEEP inside their collar (4 fingers in, thumb out, palm up). Grip should be past the tag at the back of the collar.
With the other hand, reach across to the OTHER side of the collar, gripping deep (4 fingers in, palm down). Your wrists now form an X at their throat.
Pull your elbows toward your OWN ribs (NOT flaring out). Squeeze your forearms together like closing scissors. Keep posture broken with leg pressure.
Expand your chest forward while squeezing elbows down. The pressure comes from your wrist bones on the carotid arteries — this is a blood choke, not windpipe.
complexpieces: Use the first choke grip attempt as a setup: if they posture to defend, switch to hip bump sweep or armbar
Set up from an overhook position for a deeper first grip entry
Feed the collar grip from the back of the head around the neck for even deeper placement
simplifications: Practice getting just the FIRST grip deep, repeatedly — grip depth is everything
Drill posture-breaking: pulling opponent's head down with collar grip + leg squeeze
Use a gi hanging on a bar to practice the X-choke hand position and squeeze mechanics

techniques.name: Standing Guard Break + Toreando Pass
techniques.goal: Open the opponent's closed guard by standing up and breaking their ankle lock, then immediately pass using a toreando (bullfighter) pass to side control.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent's closed guard. Strong upright posture — head up, back straight, chest out.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's pants at the knee line, or controlling their hips/belt. Hands are anchored on their body.
initialPosition.legs: One foot steps up flat on the mat near their hip (combat base). Then other foot stands up. End in a standing position inside their guard.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and UP as you stand, using hip extension to break their ankle lock.

greenLights: You've established strong posture (head up, back straight, not hunched)
You have solid grips on their pants or hips before standing
Opponent doesn't have deep collar grips pulling you down
Your balance is stable with one foot already up in combat base
redLights: Opponent has deep collar grips breaking your posture (strip grips first)
You're leaned forward with head down (vulnerable to sweeps and submissions)
Opponent has an overhook or high guard locking you in
Your balance is compromised — about to be swept
steps: Establish posture: head up, back straight. Grip both pant legs at the knees. Step one foot up near their hip (combat base), then stand up with the other, driving hips forward to break their ankle lock.
Once guard opens, immediately control both legs at the pant cuffs or ankles. Push one leg DOWN to the mat while directing the other leg across (toreando motion).
Explode laterally like a bullfighter — move your hips quickly to the side, passing their legs. Keep pressure on their legs to prevent reguarding.
Land in side control: near arm under their head (crossface), far arm under their hip. Drop chest-to-chest pressure, sprawl your legs back.
complexpieces: If opponent blocks with a knee shield, switch immediately to a knee slice pass
Combine with leg drag: as you clear the legs, drag their top leg across your body
Fake toreando one direction, then switch sides when they react
simplifications: Practice the standing guard break separately (just breaking the guard, reset)
Solo footwork drills: practice the toreando lateral movement without a partner
Start from an already-open guard to practice just the toreando passing mechanics

techniques.name: Double Under Pass
techniques.goal: Pass guard by getting both arms under opponent's legs, stacking their hips over their shoulders, and walking around to side control.

initialPosition.body: Kneeling between opponent's open guard (guard must be open). Posture upright, chest forward.
initialPosition.grips: Both arms reach under opponent's legs — biceps under their hamstrings. Hands clasp together (gable grip) behind their lower back or at their belt.
initialPosition.legs: Knees on the mat, feet together, tight base. One foot steps up when you begin the stack.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD, stacking opponent's hips over their head.

greenLights: Opponent plays open guard with feet on your hips or biceps (you can swim under both legs)
Both of opponent's legs are accessible — not locked in closed guard
Opponent is relatively flat on their back (easier to stack)
You can clasp your hands behind their back or belt
redLights: Opponent has strong cross collar grips (can set up chokes as you stack)
Opponent is very flexible — stacking has minimal effect on their mobility
You can only get under one leg (need both for double under)
Opponent is framing hard on your hips preventing you from closing distance
steps: From open guard, drive forward and swim both arms under opponent's thighs. Clasp your hands together (gable grip) behind their lower back or belt.
Step one foot up and drive forward, stacking their hips OVER their head. Your shoulder drives into their thighs. Keep your head on one side (the side you'll pass to).
Walk around to the side where your head is, maintaining constant forward stacking pressure. Don't let them unstack or create space.
Once past the legs, release the stack and drop into side control. Secure crossface and hip control immediately.
complexpieces: If they grab your collar, transition to over-under pass by switching one arm over one leg
Chain with toreando: if they kick out of the stack, immediately switch to toreando
São Paulo pass variation: grip collar instead of clasping hands, drive head to the mat
simplifications: Practice the stacking motion with just shoulder pressure (no hands) to learn the body drive
Drill getting the double underhook grip vs a static open guard
Focus on stepping UP with one foot to generate stacking power — most common mistake is staying on both knees

techniques.name: Flower Sweep (Pendulum Sweep)
techniques.goal: Sweep opponent from closed guard by swinging your leg in a wide pendulum arc while controlling their arm, using momentum to roll them over.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Hips angled slightly toward the sweeping side.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side hand grips opponent's sleeve or wrist (the arm you're controlling to prevent posting). Other hand grips behind the opponent's elbow or grabs the back of their gi pants at the knee on the same side.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Sweeping-side leg swings wide and high in a pendulum motion. Other leg hooks under opponent's far leg for leverage.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted slightly to the side, creating the angle for the pendulum swing.

greenLights: Opponent is kneeling with upright posture
You've isolated one arm with a solid sleeve/wrist grip
Opponent's weight is centered or slightly forward
You can reach their same-side pant leg at the knee
redLights: Opponent has a very wide base with knees spread far apart
Opponent is standing — pendulum motion loses effectiveness
You can't control their posting arm (they'll block the sweep)
Opponent has heavy low pressure pinning your hips flat
steps: From closed guard, secure a sleeve/wrist grip on one side and grip their pants at the knee on the same side. Open your guard.
Swing your same-side leg OUT wide and HIGH in a big pendulum arc — generate momentum with this sweeping motion.
Simultaneously PULL the sleeve grip across to prevent posting, and LIFT their knee with your pant grip. Your other leg hooks under their far thigh for added leverage.
Follow the pendulum momentum to roll them over. Come up to mount, maintaining grips throughout the transition.
complexpieces: If opponent posts their free hand, switch to an omoplata on the posted arm
Chain with scissor sweep: attempt flower sweep, if blocked, immediately set up scissor sweep
Use the pendulum motion to create angle for triangle or armbar if the sweep fails
simplifications: Practice the pendulum leg swing solo to build the movement pattern
Use double pant grips instead of sleeve + pants for easier grip setup
Start with partner giving zero resistance to learn the arc and timing

techniques.name: Overhead Sweep
techniques.goal: Sweep opponent by pulling them forward and using your legs to elevate them overhead, flipping them behind you to reverse the position.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Pulling opponent's posture down and forward.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's sleeves, collar, or belt — pulling them forward. Grips should facilitate pulling their weight over you.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Feet placed on opponent's hips or thighs, ready to extend and elevate.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted under opponent, creating space to load them onto your feet for the elevation.

greenLights: Opponent is leaning forward with their weight over you
You've successfully broken their posture and pulled them close
Opponent has narrow base with knees close together
You can plant your feet on their hips for the elevation
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture with hips back
Opponent has a very wide base making elevation difficult
You can't load their weight onto your feet
Opponent is standing up in your guard
steps: From closed guard, break opponent's posture and pull them forward using sleeve, collar, or belt grips. Get their weight shifting over you.
Open your guard and place both feet on their hips. Scoot your hips slightly under them to load their weight onto your feet.
EXTEND your legs to elevate them overhead while PULLING with your grips. Guide them over your head in a controlled arc.
Follow through, rolling backward and coming up on top. Establish top position immediately.
complexpieces: If opponent bases with their hands, transition to armbar or triangle on the posted arm
Chain with hip bump: attempt hip bump first, when they resist forward, use momentum for overhead sweep
Use butterfly hooks variation for a more controlled elevation
simplifications: Practice the leg extension and elevation motion with a light partner
Drill the hip scoot to load their weight onto your feet as a separate movement
Start from an already-broken posture position to focus on the sweep mechanics

techniques.name: Kimura Sweep
techniques.goal: Use a kimura grip (figure-four on the arm) from closed guard to break opponent's posture and sweep them when they defend the submission.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Sitting up slightly to secure the kimura grip, then using it to drive opponent over.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura figure-four grip: one hand grabs opponent's wrist, other hand reaches over their arm and grabs your own wrist. Locked tight.
initialPosition.legs: Guard stays closed or opens slightly to create hip angle for the sweep direction.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the kimura grip side, using the grip as a lever to direct the sweep.

greenLights: Opponent posts their hand on the mat beside you — wrist is available for kimura grip
You've secured the figure-four kimura grip on their arm
Opponent is trying to pull their arm free and creating backward momentum
Opponent's posture is broken and they're not defending the grip
redLights: Opponent keeps elbows tight to their body — can't isolate an arm
Opponent grabs their own belt or lapel to defend the kimura grip
You don't have a solid figure-four lock yet
Opponent has strong upright posture and you can't reach their arm
steps: From closed guard, when opponent posts a hand on the mat, grab their wrist with your same-side hand. Reach your other hand OVER their arm and grab your own wrist — figure-four kimura grip.
Sit up slightly and pull their elbow tight to your body. Control the arm close to your chest.
Open your guard slightly, angle your hips, and use the kimura grip as a lever to drive them sideways. Kick with your legs in the sweep direction.
Follow through to mount or side control. You can either finish the kimura from top position or release and establish dominant position.
complexpieces: If they resist the sweep, finish the kimura submission from the bottom
Chain with hip bump: attempt hip bump, when they post their hand, catch the kimura grip
Transition to guillotine if they duck their head while defending the kimura
simplifications: Practice securing the kimura grip quickly from closed guard — speed is key
Drill the figure-four grip mechanics separately until automatic
Work the sweep against a cooperative partner to learn the leverage angle

techniques.name: Sit-Up Sweep
techniques.goal: Sit up into your opponent and drive forward to off-balance and sweep them backward. Similar to hip bump but with more emphasis on forward drive and control.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from lying on back to sitting up into opponent. Driving chest-to-chest.
initialPosition.grips: One hand posts on the mat behind you for the sit-up. Other hand controls opponent's arm, collar, or reaches for an underhook.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens as you sit up. One foot plants on the mat for driving power.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and upward into opponent's center of gravity.

greenLights: Opponent postures up with their weight shifting backward
Opponent's hands are off you momentarily
You have space to sit up explosively
Opponent has narrow base or their weight is centered
redLights: Opponent has heavy forward pressure with head low
Opponent has deep collar grips controlling your posture
You're pinned flat with no ability to sit up
Opponent is actively working a pass
steps: When opponent postures up, post one hand behind you on the mat and EXPLOSIVELY sit up toward them.
Drive your chest into their chest, reaching for an underhook or collar grip with your free arm.
Open your guard and plant your foot on the mat for added drive. Push forward and off-balance them backward.
Drive through to top position. Establish mount or side control immediately.
complexpieces: If they push you back down, transition to guillotine as they lean forward
Chain with kimura: sit up, when they post to resist, catch the kimura grip
Combine with hip bump — the sit-up creates the angle for a more powerful bump
simplifications: Practice the explosive sit-up motion solo (just the movement, no partner)
Focus on the timing — sit up the instant they posture up
Drill against a static partner to learn the forward drive path

techniques.name: Clamp Sweep
techniques.goal: Control the opponent's arm and clamp their shoulder while using your legs to rotate them over. Uses upper body clamping pressure combined with leg drive.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Upper body clamping opponent's arm and shoulder tight to your chest.
initialPosition.grips: Both arms clamp opponent's arm tight to your chest — overhook their arm and hug it tight. Control their shoulder to prevent them from posturing.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Legs work to create rotational force in the sweep direction.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the clamped arm side, creating the rotational vector for the sweep.

greenLights: You've successfully clamped their arm tight to your chest with an overhook
Opponent's shoulder is pinned and they can't posture up
Opponent is reaching across or leaving an arm exposed
Their base is compromised on the clamped side
redLights: Opponent pulls their arm free from the clamp — you lose the lever
Opponent has a very wide base on both sides
You can't secure the overhook to clamp the shoulder
Opponent is posturing strongly and you can't break them down
steps: From closed guard, secure an overhook on opponent's arm and CLAMP it tight to your chest. Hug their shoulder with both arms.
Angle your hips toward the clamped arm side. Their posting ability on that side is eliminated.
Open your guard and use your legs to create rotational momentum — drive your legs toward the clamped side to roll them over.
Follow through to top position. Maintain the clamp control as you establish mount or side control.
complexpieces: If they resist the sweep, transition to omoplata from the overhook position
Chain with triangle: the overhook creates the arm-in position for a triangle setup
Use the clamp to set up a wrist lock on the trapped arm
simplifications: Practice the overhook and shoulder clamp as an isolated drill
Focus on the hip angle change with the clamp secured before adding leg drive
Drill with a cooperative partner to learn the rotational mechanics

techniques.name: Lumberjack Sweep
techniques.goal: When opponent stands up in your closed guard, control both ankles and push-pull to sweep them backward. Capitalizes on the standing guard break posture.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back. Opponent has stood up inside your guard to attempt a guard break.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands grab opponent's ankles or pant cuffs at the feet. Control both legs.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Feet placed on opponent's hips or inner thighs, ready to push.
initialPosition.hips: On the mat, ready to push with feet while pulling ankles.

greenLights: Opponent stands up in your guard — this is the trigger for this sweep
You can reach both of their ankles or pant legs
Opponent's weight is shifting backward as they try to open your guard
Opponent's hands are on your legs/hips and not defending their base
redLights: Opponent has already opened your guard and stepped to the side
You can only reach one ankle — need to control both
Opponent has strong forward posture and isn't leaning back
Opponent is actively passing and has cleared your legs
steps: As opponent stands up in your guard, open your guard and immediately grab BOTH ankles or pant cuffs.
Place your feet on their hips or inner thighs. Push with your feet while simultaneously PULLING their ankles toward you.
The push-pull motion takes their feet out from under them — they fall backward.
Immediately come up on top as they fall. Establish a passing position or drop into side control.
complexpieces: If they step one leg back, transition to a single leg or De La Riva hook
Chain with ankle pick: if they resist the sweep, pull one ankle harder for an ankle pick takedown
Use the lumberjack grip to transition to X-guard or single leg X
simplifications: Practice the timing of grabbing ankles as soon as they stand — reaction drill
Drill the push-pull motion with feet on hips against a standing partner
Start with partner already standing to focus on the sweep mechanics

techniques.name: Arm Drag to Back Take
techniques.goal: Use an arm drag from closed guard to pull the opponent forward and around your body, exposing their back and allowing you to secure back control.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Ready to sit up slightly and rotate as you drag their arm.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands grip opponent's same-side wrist and tricep area. One hand controls the wrist, the other cups the tricep to pull.
initialPosition.legs: Guard stays closed initially, then opens to allow you to rotate behind them.
initialPosition.hips: Scooting to the side as you drag, creating angle to access their back.

greenLights: Opponent extends an arm toward you — pushing on chest or reaching for collar
You've secured a strong two-on-one grip on their arm
Opponent's weight is forward and they're not defending their back
You can scoot your hips to create the angle for the back take
redLights: Opponent keeps elbows tight and doesn't extend arms
Opponent is sitting back with weight away from you
You can't get a two-on-one grip on their arm
Opponent immediately pulls their arm back when you grab it
steps: From closed guard, grab opponent's wrist with one hand and cup their tricep with the other — two-on-one grip on the same arm.
DRAG their arm across your body while simultaneously scooting your hips to the opposite side. Sit up as you drag.
As their arm clears past your body, their back is exposed. Open your guard and start climbing to their back.
Secure a seatbelt grip (one arm over shoulder, one under armpit) and insert your hooks. Establish full back control.
complexpieces: If they resist the drag, use the two-on-one to set up a triangle or armbar
Chain with collar drag for a similar back take from a different grip
If you can't get to the back, transition to a single leg takedown from the seated position
simplifications: Practice the arm drag motion (two-on-one pull) as an isolated grip drill
Drill the hip scoot and rotation without finishing the back take
Start with partner extending their arm to focus on the drag mechanics

techniques.name: Muscle Sweep
techniques.goal: A powerful sweep from closed guard that uses upper body strength and timing to elevate and reverse the opponent when they resist posture breaking.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Using upper body strength to drive opponent over.
initialPosition.grips: Strong collar and sleeve grips, or double underhooks controlling opponent's upper body. Grips facilitate the powerful lifting motion.
initialPosition.legs: Guard closed tight, squeezing to break posture. Legs assist the sweep by driving in the sweep direction.
initialPosition.hips: Loaded and ready to bridge powerfully into the sweep direction.

greenLights: Opponent is resisting your posture break by driving into you — use their energy
You have strong upper body grips or underhooks secured
Opponent's weight is centered or slightly forward
You have the strength and explosiveness to power through the sweep
redLights: Opponent has a very wide stable base
You don't have strong enough grips to control the sweep
Opponent is sitting back with weight away from you
You're exhausted and can't generate explosive power
steps: From closed guard, secure strong upper body grips — deep collar grip and sleeve, or fight for double underhooks.
Break opponent's posture down. When they resist and push back into you, use that forward energy.
Bridge explosively and use your grips to elevate and direct them to the side. Combine hip bridge with leg drive.
Follow through the bridge momentum to reverse to top position. Establish mount or side control.
complexpieces: Chain with hip bump: use the muscle sweep attempt to set up a hip bump when they resist
If they post a hand, switch to kimura on the posted arm
Combine with scissor sweep mechanics for added leverage
simplifications: Practice the explosive bridge with direction change as a solo drill
Drill the grip placement and upper body control separately
Work against a cooperative partner to learn the correct elevation angle

techniques.name: Guillotine Choke
techniques.goal: Wrap your arm around the opponent's neck and apply a choke by squeezing upward while controlling them with your closed guard. Attacks when opponent dives their head down.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Opponent's head is down beside your chest — they've ducked their head low.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm wraps around opponent's neck — wrist bone or blade of forearm against their throat/neck. Same hand grabs your other hand (palm-to-palm or gable grip) for reinforcement.
initialPosition.legs: Guard closed tight, pulling opponent into you. Can switch to high guard (legs climbing higher on their back) for a tighter finish.
initialPosition.hips: Arching upward, driving the blade of your forearm into their neck. Hips provide the finishing pressure.

greenLights: Opponent ducks their head to your chest trying to pass or posture — neck is exposed
You've wrapped your arm around their neck with the forearm blade on the throat
Opponent's head is below your shoulder line and you can cinch the grip
Opponent shoots forward into you (takedown attempt or posture break)
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture — can't reach around their neck
Your arm is around the chin, not the neck — jaw choke, not effective
Opponent has already passed to the side — guillotine loses leverage
You can't close your hands together for the reinforced grip
steps: When opponent ducks their head low, wrap your near arm around the FRONT of their neck. Blade of your wrist/forearm against their throat.
Grab your choking hand with your free hand — palm-to-palm or gable grip. Pull their head tight to your chest.
Close your guard HIGH on their back. Squeeze your knees together and pull them in tight.
Arch your hips UP and squeeze your arms together. The upward hip pressure combined with the arm squeeze compresses the neck for the choke.
complexpieces: If they posture out, transition to a high-elbow guillotine by angling the choking arm upward
Chain with hip bump sweep: sit up for hip bump, when they duck, catch the guillotine
If they pull their head out, switch to an anaconda or d'arce choke
simplifications: Practice the wrap-around grip on a partner's neck to find the correct blade placement
Drill the hip arch finishing motion without the neck wrap to learn the body mechanics
Start with opponent's head already lowered to focus on the grip and finish

techniques.name: Kimura from Closed Guard
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's shoulder with a figure-four grip on their arm from closed guard, rotating their wrist behind their back against the shoulder joint.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Sitting up slightly to one side to secure the grip and create angle.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura figure-four grip: one hand grabs opponent's wrist, other hand reaches OVER their arm and grabs your own wrist. Grip locked tight against your chest.
initialPosition.legs: Guard open or closed. If open, top leg presses on their back for control.
initialPosition.hips: Angled to the side of the kimura grip, scooted slightly off-center.

greenLights: Opponent posts their hand on the mat beside you — wrist is exposed
You've secured the figure-four kimura grip on their arm
Opponent's arm is separated from their body
Opponent is focused on posturing and not defending the arm
redLights: Opponent grabs their own belt, lapel, or pants to defend (break the grip first)
Opponent keeps elbows extremely tight to their body
You can't get your hand over their arm for the figure-four
Opponent is stacking you and you're losing angle
steps: From closed guard, when opponent posts a hand on the mat, grab their wrist with your same-side hand.
Sit up slightly, reach your other hand OVER their arm, and grab your own wrist — figure-four kimura grip secured.
Pull their elbow tight to your chest. Open your guard and place your top leg over their back for added control.
Pin their forearm down and rotate their wrist toward their back (behind their spine). Apply smooth rotational pressure on the shoulder joint.
complexpieces: If they resist the kimura, use the grip to sweep them (kimura sweep)
Chain with hip bump: attempt hip bump, they post hand, catch the kimura
If they straighten the arm to defend, switch to a straight armbar
simplifications: Practice securing the figure-four grip quickly as a reaction drill
Drill the wrist rotation finish from a static position
Focus on the sit-up and angle change before adding the submission

techniques.name: Omoplata
techniques.goal: Use your legs to isolate and attack the opponent's shoulder joint from closed guard. Your leg wraps over their shoulder and rotates the arm behind their back.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard, transitioning to a perpendicular angle. Rotating your body 90° relative to opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side sleeve or wrist grip on the arm you're attacking. Other hand on their collar or head to control posture.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. Attacking leg swings HIGH over opponent's shoulder and wraps over their back. Other foot pushes on their hip for the pivot.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to create a perpendicular angle to opponent's body — similar angle to an armbar setup.

greenLights: Opponent extends an arm — pushing, posting, or reaching for collar
You've created a perpendicular angle with your hips
Opponent's posture is broken and they can't retract their arm quickly
Failed triangle attempt where they pull the arm out — perfect omoplata transition
redLights: Opponent keeps both arms tight and doesn't extend
Opponent has strong upright posture and you can't throw your leg over
Opponent is already rolling forward to escape before you can control their hips
You can't create enough angle to get your leg over their shoulder
steps: From closed guard, control one arm at the wrist/sleeve. Push their head to the opposite side with your other hand.
Open guard, place one foot on their hip, and pivot your hips to create a perpendicular angle. Swing your attacking leg HIGH over their shoulder.
Sit up and control their hip/belt to prevent the forward roll escape. Your leg is draped over their shoulder with their arm trapped.
Lean forward, driving their arm behind their back using your leg pressure. Control their hip and apply rotational pressure on the shoulder for the submission.
complexpieces: If they roll forward to escape, follow the roll and sweep to top position
Chain from triangle: if triangle fails and they pull the arm out, transition to omoplata
Set up a wrist lock on the trapped arm while holding the omoplata position
simplifications: Practice the hip pivot and leg-over-shoulder motion as an isolated movement drill
Drill sitting up and controlling the hip — the most common mistake is lying flat
Break into phases: (1) angle creation, (2) leg placement, (3) sit-up and control, (4) finish

techniques.name: Loop Choke
techniques.goal: Feed the opponent's collar behind their neck and create a tight choking loop. A sneaky collar choke that catches opponents off guard during transitions.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard or transitioning as opponent tries to pass. Body angled toward the choking side.
initialPosition.grips: Lead hand feeds deep into opponent's cross collar — fingers inside the collar, thumb outside. Pull the collar across the back of their neck to create the loop. Other hand controls their posture or blocks their arm.
initialPosition.legs: Guard closed or transitioning. Legs pull opponent's posture down to tighten the collar loop.
initialPosition.hips: Angled slightly toward the choking grip side.

greenLights: You've fed a deep collar grip across the back of their neck
Opponent is driving forward into you with head low
Opponent is trying to pass guard and not defending the collar
You can create the collar loop around the back of their neck
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture — can't feed the collar deep enough
Opponent is actively grip-fighting your collar grip
You can't reach deep enough into the collar for an effective loop
Opponent is pulling away and creating distance
steps: From closed guard, feed your hand DEEP into their cross collar (4 fingers inside). Pull the collar across and behind their neck to create a loop around the back of their head.
With your other hand, control their posture — grip behind their head, their sleeve, or block their posting arm.
Close your guard tight and pull them DOWN. The collar loop tightens around their neck as they try to posture up.
Squeeze by pulling the collar grip toward your chest while keeping their posture broken. The loop acts as a noose around the neck arteries.
complexpieces: Set up during a guard pass attempt — feed the collar as they try to open your guard
Chain with cross collar choke: if the loop choke fails, the grip is already set for the second hand
Use the collar control to sweep if the choke isn't finishing
simplifications: Practice feeding the collar grip deep behind the neck repeatedly — depth is everything
Drill the loop formation on a static partner or gi on a hanger
Focus on the posture break with legs — the choke tightens when they can't posture

techniques.name: Gogoplata
techniques.goal: Use your shin across the opponent's throat while controlling their posture from closed guard, creating a choke with leg pressure against the windpipe and arteries.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Transitioning to rubber guard or high guard — one leg climbs very high on opponent's back.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands pull opponent's head DOWN toward your chest. Controlling the back of their head is critical to keep the shin in place.
initialPosition.legs: One shin placed directly across the front of opponent's throat. Other leg wraps behind their back or head for control.
initialPosition.hips: Elevated and angled, with significant flexibility required to place shin on throat.

greenLights: You have excellent hip flexibility — this is an advanced flexibility technique
Opponent's posture is fully broken and they can't lift their head
You've successfully placed your shin across their throat
Opponent is focused on escaping other submissions and not defending the shin
redLights: You don't have the flexibility to get your shin to their throat — don't force it
Opponent has strong posture and can lift their head away from your shin
Your shin placement is on the chin, not the throat — ineffective
Opponent is stacking you and your hips can't maintain the position
steps: From closed guard, break opponent's posture completely. Get rubber guard or high guard control — pull one leg very high on their back.
Feed your foot through and place your shin across the FRONT of their throat. Your shin bone presses into their neck.
Grab the back of their head with BOTH hands and pull their face DOWN into your shin. This closes the choking pressure.
Squeeze by pulling their head down and pressing your shin forward. The shin compresses the throat and arteries.
complexpieces: Transition from failed omoplata — if the leg is already over their shoulder, redirect the shin to the throat
Set up from rubber guard: use the rubber guard to walk the leg into position
If they escape, transition to triangle or omoplata with the leg already in position
simplifications: Practice hip flexibility drills extensively before attempting this technique
Drill the shin placement against a cooperative partner without finishing the choke
Work from rubber guard position first to develop the high-leg control needed

techniques.name: Baratoplata
techniques.goal: An advanced shoulder lock that combines armbar and omoplata mechanics from closed guard. Traps the opponent's arm in a unique configuration using your legs and arms together.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Transitioning through an omoplata-like position with additional arm control.
initialPosition.grips: Figure-four or reverse grip controlling opponent's wrist and forearm. One arm threads through their trapped arm for additional lock control.
initialPosition.legs: Guard opens. One leg wraps over opponent's shoulder similar to omoplata. The leg and arm control create a compound lock.
initialPosition.hips: Angled perpendicular to opponent, similar to omoplata positioning.

greenLights: Opponent's arm is isolated and you have wrist control
You've achieved a perpendicular angle similar to omoplata position
Failed omoplata or armbar attempt — baratoplata is a transition option
Opponent's arm is bent and you can thread your grip through
redLights: You're not familiar with the mechanics — this is a very advanced technique
Opponent's arm is straight — the compound lock requires a bent arm
You can't create the perpendicular angle needed
Opponent is already escaping and you don't have secure control
steps: From closed guard, isolate opponent's arm using an omoplata-type setup — swing your leg over their shoulder and create a perpendicular angle.
Instead of finishing the omoplata, thread your same-side arm UNDER their trapped forearm and grip your own wrist or their wrist — creating a compound figure-four lock.
Your leg over their shoulder combined with the arm thread creates a double lock on the shoulder and elbow.
Apply pressure by rotating their arm behind their back while your leg and arm work together as a compound lever. Smooth, controlled pressure.
complexpieces: Chain from omoplata: when they defend the omoplata by clasping hands, thread the baratoplata grip
Transition from armbar: if they bend their arm to defend the armbar, wrap into baratoplata
Use as a surprise transition when opponent thinks they've defended the initial attack
simplifications: Master the omoplata position first — baratoplata builds on the same angle and leg placement
Drill the arm-threading grip mechanic slowly against a cooperative partner
Break into phases: (1) achieve omoplata angle, (2) thread the arm grip, (3) apply compound pressure

techniques.name: Wrist Lock from Closed Guard
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's wrist when they post or defend submissions from your closed guard. A subtle joint lock that capitalizes on their defensive reactions.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in closed guard. Often set up as opponent defends another submission and their wrist is exposed.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands control opponent's hand and forearm. One hand grabs the back of their hand (fingers wrapping over the knuckles), other hand controls their forearm.
initialPosition.legs: Guard closed, pulling opponent down to reduce their ability to retract the arm.
initialPosition.hips: Slightly angled to create the leverage needed for the wrist bend.

greenLights: Opponent posts their hand on the mat — wrist is vulnerable
Opponent grabs your collar or gi with a stiff arm — hand is accessible
They're pushing away on your chest with an extended arm
Opponent is defending another submission and leaving their wrist exposed
redLights: Opponent's wrists are flexible and they can absorb the pressure
You can't control both the hand and forearm for proper leverage
Opponent immediately retracts their arm when you grab the hand
The angle is wrong — you're bending the wrist in a direction with no joint lock
steps: Identify the exposed wrist — often when opponent posts, pushes, or grabs your gi with a stiff arm.
With one hand, grip the BACK of their hand (knuckles facing you). With the other hand, control their forearm just above the wrist.
Bend their wrist by pushing their hand toward their forearm — curl the hand over itself. Use your body and grips as the lever.
Apply steady, controlled pressure. The wrist joint has limited range — even small angles create significant submission pressure. Control the forearm so they can't pull out.
complexpieces: Set up from armbar defense: when they clasp hands to defend an armbar, attack the wrist
Chain from collar grip attempt: when they grab your collar with a stiff arm, catch the wrist lock
Use as a secondary attack during any submission attempt where their hand is exposed
simplifications: Practice the hand-over-forearm bending mechanics slowly — wrist locks are subtle
Drill identifying the exposed wrist in various defensive positions
Start with partner posting their hand to focus on the grip and angle

techniques.name: Knee-in-Tailbone Guard Break
techniques.goal: Break opponent's closed guard by placing your knee in their tailbone area and applying pressure to pry their ankles apart. A kneeling guard break that doesn't require standing.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent's closed guard, kneeling. Strong upright posture — head up, back straight, chest out.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's collar, lapels, or controlling their hips. Hands anchored for posture and control.
initialPosition.legs: One knee steps up and drives into opponent's tailbone area. Other knee stays on the mat for base.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and downward, using the knee to create splitting pressure on their guard lock.

greenLights: You've established strong posture inside the guard
You can drive your knee into the tailbone gap between their legs
Opponent's ankles are getting tired from holding the guard closed
You have solid lapel or hip grips for control
redLights: Opponent has deep collar grips breaking your posture — strip grips first
You can't establish upright posture (leaned forward = vulnerable to attacks)
Opponent has very strong guard lock and your knee can't find the tailbone
Opponent is actively attacking submissions — defend first
steps: Establish posture inside closed guard: head up, back straight. Grip both lapels or control their hips.
Step one knee up and drive it into the gap near opponent's tailbone/coccyx area. Press your shin against their inner thigh.
Drive your hips forward while pushing your knee down — this creates a splitting pressure that forces their ankles apart.
As the guard opens, immediately control their legs and begin your pass. Don't let them reguard.
complexpieces: Combine with standing guard break: if the knee pressure doesn't work, transition to standing
Immediately chain into toreando or knee slice pass once the guard opens
Use collar grips to push them away as the guard breaks for extra distance
simplifications: Practice posture establishment in closed guard first — posture is the foundation
Drill the knee placement and hip drive against a partner holding light guard pressure
Focus on the timing — drive forward steadily, don't jerk or bounce

techniques.name: Log Splitter Guard Break
techniques.goal: Control both lapels or opponent's torso and drive your knee between their legs to split the guard open. A forceful kneeling guard break using wedge pressure.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent's closed guard, kneeling with strong upright posture. Chest out, head up.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's lapels, gi, or controlling their torso. Pulling upward and toward you for posture.
initialPosition.legs: One knee drives between opponent's thighs, splitting through like a wedge. Other knee provides base.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward, using hip extension and the wedge knee to force the guard open.

greenLights: You have strong posture with good lapel or torso control
You can wedge your knee between their thighs effectively
Opponent's guard lock isn't extremely tight
You have good base and balance to drive forward
redLights: Opponent has very tight guard lock that won't yield to the wedge
Your posture is broken — you're leaned forward and vulnerable
Opponent has deep collar grips controlling you
You're off-balance and the knee drive would compromise your base
steps: Establish strong posture inside closed guard. Grip both lapels or control their torso by pulling upward.
Drive one knee forward BETWEEN their thighs like a wedge. Use a log-splitting motion — steady, forward, splitting pressure.
Extend your hips forward while driving the wedge knee deeper. The splitting pressure forces their ankles apart and opens the guard.
As the guard breaks, immediately establish leg control and transition to your preferred guard pass.
complexpieces: Chain with toreando pass: once guard opens, immediately redirect to toreando
If the log splitter stalls, switch to standing guard break
Use lapel grips to push opponent away as guard breaks for added distance
simplifications: Practice the knee wedge motion against light resistance first
Focus on steady forward hip pressure rather than explosive bursts
Drill the posture and grip setup separately before adding the knee drive

techniques.name: Sao Paulo Pass
techniques.goal: Apply strong shoulder pressure while controlling the legs to pass after opening the guard. A pressure-based pass that uses heavy upper body weight to pin and pass.

initialPosition.body: Guard has been opened. Driving heavy shoulder pressure into opponent while controlling their legs.
initialPosition.grips: One hand controls opponent's collar (pulling their head up), other hand controls their pant leg or hip. Shoulder drives into their thigh or chest.
initialPosition.legs: Strong base — one foot up, other knee on the mat. Driving forward with bodyweight.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and low, driving downward pressure through your shoulder into opponent.

greenLights: Guard has been successfully opened
You can drive heavy shoulder pressure into opponent
You have good collar and pant grips for control
Opponent is pinned flat and can't create frames
redLights: Guard is still closed — open it first before attempting this pass
Opponent has strong frames preventing your shoulder pressure
You can't anchor collar and pant grips
Opponent is actively reguarding and your shoulder pressure is ineffective
steps: After opening the guard, immediately drive your shoulder INTO opponent's thigh or chest. Establish heavy pressure.
Grip their collar with one hand (pulling their head toward you) and their pant leg with the other. Anchor your grips.
Walk around toward their head, maintaining constant shoulder driving pressure. Don't let up — keep them pinned flat.
Clear the legs and drop into side control. Secure crossface and hip control immediately.
complexpieces: Chain with double under pass: if they frame against the Sao Paulo, switch to double unders
Use the collar grip to set up a choke during the pass transition
If they reguard one leg, transition to half guard passing (knee slice)
simplifications: Practice driving shoulder pressure into a partner from a static position
Drill the walk-around footwork without resistance
Start with guard already open to focus on the pressure passing mechanics

techniques.name: Menotte Closed Guard Pass (Mako)
techniques.goal: Use the menotte (handcuff-style arm control) to trap the opponent’s arms together, break posture safely, open the closed guard, and pass while they are immobilized.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent’s closed guard with upright posture. Opponent attempting to control sleeves or collar.
initialPosition.grips: Secure a two-on-one style control that brings one of their arms across their centerline and traps it against their other arm (menotte configuration). Hands connect palm-to-palm or gable grip to immobilize both arms.
initialPosition.legs: Stable base — knees wide, toes active. Hips heavy and centered to prevent sweeps.
initialPosition.hips: Neutral and balanced. Avoid leaning too far forward before arm control is secured.

greenLights: You successfully trap one arm across their body
Their hands are pinned together (menotte secured)
Opponent cannot effectively frame or collar grip
Their posture is compromised and shoulders are flat
redLights: Posture is broken before securing the menotte
Opponent has strong sleeve/collar control preventing arm drag
You lean forward without arm control (risk of triangle/armbar)
Opponent opens guard first and creates angle
steps: From inside closed guard, isolate one of their arms and drag it across their centerline.
Trap that arm against their other arm, securing a tight menotte (handcuff-style) grip so both arms are immobilized.
Apply forward shoulder pressure to pin their arms toward their chest while maintaining strong posture and base.
Stand or wedge a knee to begin opening the closed guard while keeping the menotte tight.
Once the guard opens, immediately angle off toward the trapped-arm side and pass (knee slide or walk-around) while their arms remain controlled.
Settle into side control, maintaining upper-body dominance before releasing the grip.
complexpieces: Transition directly to arm triangle if their arms stay compressed
Chain into knee slide pass once guard opens
If they resist the arm drag, switch to standard arm drag to back take
simplifications: Drill the arm-drag-to-menotte transition from seated guard first
Practice holding the menotte while partner gives light resistance
Start with guard loosely closed to focus on arm trapping mechanics

techniques.name: Carlson Gracie Jr. Sitting Guard Break
techniques.goal: Sit back with strong posture while controlling lapels or biceps, widen your knees for base, and apply steady outward pressure with elbows and hips to force the guard open.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent's closed guard, sitting back on your heels with very strong upright posture.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands controlling opponent's lapels, biceps, or belt. Elbows drive outward into their inner thighs.
initialPosition.legs: Knees widened significantly for a very solid base. Sitting on your heels, hips back.
initialPosition.hips: Sitting back and driving outward — creating expanding pressure against their guard lock.

greenLights: You can establish strong upright posture sitting back on your heels
Your knees are wide enough to create a stable base
You have good lapel or bicep grips for control
Opponent's guard lock is starting to weaken from the outward pressure
redLights: Opponent has deep collar grips pulling your posture forward
You can't sit back — they're holding you forward with grips
Your base is too narrow and you're off-balance sitting back
Opponent is actively attacking and you need to defend first
steps: Establish strong posture inside closed guard. Grip both lapels or control their biceps. Sit back on your heels.
Widen your knees significantly to create a broad, stable base. Bring your elbows to the INSIDE of their thighs.
Drive your elbows and hips OUTWARD — creating a steady expanding pressure against their closed guard lock. Don't rush — use steady pressure.
As the guard opens, immediately control their legs and transition to your preferred guard pass.
complexpieces: Chain with standing guard break: if the seated break stalls, switch to standing
Immediately transition to toreando or leg drag once the guard opens
Use the wide base position to defend any submission attempts while working the break
simplifications: Practice the seated posture with wide knees as a positional hold
Drill the outward elbow pressure against light guard resistance
Focus on patience — this guard break relies on steady pressure, not explosive movement

techniques.name: Standard Standing Closed Guard Opening
techniques.goal: Control opponent's sleeve and lapels, stand with strong posture, step one leg back, and use downward pressure on hips or thigh to pry the guard open.

initialPosition.body: Inside opponent's closed guard. Transitioning from kneeling to standing with controlled, purposeful steps.
initialPosition.grips: One hand grips opponent's sleeve (prevents them from gripping you during the stand). Other hand controls their hip, belt, or lapel. Grips anchor you during the stand-up.
initialPosition.legs: One foot steps up first (combat base), then stand fully. Step one leg back to create angle for the guard break.
initialPosition.hips: Driving upward as you stand, extending hips to pressure their ankles apart.

greenLights: You've established strong posture before standing
Your sleeve grip prevents them from pulling you forward as you stand
You can step up to combat base without being off-balanced
Opponent doesn't have deep collar grips
redLights: Opponent has deep collar grips — strip them before standing
You're being pulled forward and can't establish posture to stand
Opponent has an overhook or high guard trapping you
Your balance is compromised — don't stand until you're stable
steps: Establish posture. Grip their sleeve with one hand and control their hip or lapel with the other. Step one foot up into combat base.
Stand up fully, keeping your posture tall and hips driving forward. The sleeve grip prevents them from pulling you back down.
Step one leg BACK and push DOWN on their hip or thigh with your free hand. The angle change and downward pressure pries their ankles apart.
As the guard opens, immediately control both legs at the pant cuffs or ankles and begin your preferred pass.
complexpieces: Chain directly into toreando pass once the guard opens
If they hang on during the stand, shake the leg or circle to break the lock
Use the standing position to set up a leg drag pass immediately after opening
simplifications: Practice the stand-up from closed guard with focus on balance — no guard break, just standing
Drill the step-back and hip push as an isolated movement
Start from combat base to focus only on the standing and breaking portion

positions.name: Half Guard
positions.description: One of your legs is entangled with one of opponent's legs. Versatile position for sweeps from bottom and passes from top.

techniques.name: Underhook Sweep (Old School)
techniques.goal: Sweep from half guard bottom to top position by securing a deep underhook and driving forward into your opponent like a tackle.

initialPosition.body: On bottom in half guard, turned ON YOUR SIDE facing opponent. Never flat on your back. Head tight against opponent's hip/ribcage.
initialPosition.grips: Deep underhook on the far side — arm threaded under their armpit, hand reaching their back. Other hand controls their wrist or blocks the crossface.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard locked around their near-side leg (triangle lock or lockdown). Knees close to your chest.
initialPosition.hips: Angled on your side, hips close to opponent. Head is BELOW their chest level to deny the crossface.

greenLights: You've secured a deep underhook (arm under their armpit, hand reaching their back)
Your head is below their chest level — denying the crossface
Opponent is driving forward pressure into you (you use their momentum)
Opponent reaches for your head with their crossface arm — creates space for underhook
redLights: Opponent has a strong crossface with heavy shoulder pressure (ear pinned to mat)
You're flat on your back — no ability to generate sweep power
Opponent has a strong whizzer/overhook countering your underhook
Opponent is sitting back with weight away from you (no momentum to use)
steps: Get on your side and FIGHT for the underhook: swim your arm under their far-side armpit. Keep your head tight against their hip to deny crossface.
With underhook secured, walk your hips out slightly to create angle. Keep your half guard locked tight on their leg.
Drive FORWARD into them using the underhook like a tackle. Come up to your knees first, then to your feet, lifting and driving through.
Take them over to their back. Establish top position — side control or extract your trapped leg to pass the half guard.
complexpieces: If they whizzer hard, duck under and take the back instead of sweeping forward
Chain with dog fight: if they base out wide, transition to single leg or back take from the dog fight position
Use a knee tap with your free hand on their far knee to accelerate the sweep
simplifications: Drill just the underhook fight: start flat on back, practice getting to your side and securing the underhook
Practice the forward drive (coming up to knees) without finishing the sweep
Start with the underhook already secured to focus only on the sweep mechanics

techniques.name: Knee Shield to Guard Recovery
techniques.goal: Use the knee shield as a frame to create distance, prevent the pass, and recover to a full guard position (closed guard, butterfly, or open guard).

initialPosition.body: On bottom in half guard, turned to your side. Top knee wedged across opponent's chest or midsection as a frame.
initialPosition.grips: Near hand framing on their bicep or shoulder. Far hand on their collar, hip, or behind their head.
initialPosition.legs: Top leg: knee across their body (knee shield), foot hooked on their hip. Bottom leg: locked in half guard on their near leg.
initialPosition.hips: Angled slightly away from opponent, creating distance with the knee shield.

greenLights: You've managed to wedge your knee across their body before they flatten you
Opponent is trying to close distance and smash past — your knee shield is holding
You have frames on their upper body preventing them from collapsing
There's enough space to hip escape and start extracting your bottom leg
redLights: Opponent has already flattened you and passed around the knee shield
They have an underhook AND have passed your knee
You have no frames and they have crushing shoulder-to-face pressure
Your bottom leg is completely stapled and pinned to the mat
steps: From half guard, get on your side and wedge your top knee across opponent's belly/chest. Keep your foot hooked on their hip for structure.
Frame with both hands: near hand on their bicep/shoulder, far hand on their collar or head. PUSH to create distance.
Use the distance to hip escape (shrimp) and start extracting your bottom leg from the half guard entanglement.
Recover to full guard (lock ankles) or transition to an open guard (De La Riva, butterfly, collar-sleeve) depending on the distance.
complexpieces: From knee shield, transition directly to a scissor sweep setup — the position is very similar
Use the knee shield to set up triangle or omoplata when they try to pass around it
Drop the knee shield down to transition to X-guard or single leg X
simplifications: Drill just the knee shield insertion: from flat on back, practice getting to your side with knee across their body
Practice hip escapes (shrimps) as a separate solo drill
Start with the knee shield already in place and focus only on the guard recovery part

techniques.name: Knee Slice Pass
techniques.goal: Pass the half guard by sliding your lead knee across opponent's thigh while driving heavy crossface pressure to flatten them and advance to side control.

initialPosition.body: In combat base inside half guard (top position). Heavy shoulder pressure on the passing side.
initialPosition.grips: Near arm: crossface — arm wrapping behind their head, shoulder driving into their jaw/cheek. Far hand: gripping the far collar or underhooking their far arm.
initialPosition.legs: Lead knee placed diagonally across opponent's thigh (the slicing knee). Other leg posted back for base and drive.
initialPosition.hips: Weight heavy on the slicing knee, hips LOW and driving forward. Not up on your toes.

greenLights: You have a strong crossface with your shoulder driving into their jaw (they can't face you)
Opponent's knee shield has been cleared or flattened
You have head position on the passing side — they can't get the underhook
Their bottom leg is relatively flat and your knee can slice across easily
redLights: Opponent has a deep underhook on the passing side (they'll take your back)
Opponent has a strong knee shield you can't clear — don't force it
You DON'T have head position (crossface) — vulnerable to back takes
Opponent is framing strongly against your shoulder preventing you from closing distance
steps: Secure the crossface: drive your near-side shoulder INTO their jaw. Arm wraps behind their head. Grip the far collar for anchor control.
Clear any knee shield by driving your hips LOW and stapling their bottom leg. Place your lead knee across their thigh, pointing diagonally toward the mat.
Slice your knee across their thigh while driving FORWARD with constant chest pressure. Keep your hips LOW — do not rise up or create space.
Clear your foot free from their half guard lock. Consolidate side control with crossface and hip control. Don't rush — maintain heavy pressure throughout.
complexpieces: If opponent underhooks on the passing side, switch to a d'arce choke or backstep to leg drag
Chain with mount: after passing, immediately step over to mount
Use a collar grip to set up a baseball bat choke as you slice through
simplifications: Practice the knee slide motion against no resistance as a solo drill
Start from half guard with the crossface already secured — focus only on the knee slice
Focus first on maintaining overwhelming shoulder pressure before adding the knee slice

positions.name: Mount
positions.description: Dominant top position sitting on opponent's torso. One of the highest-value positions in BJJ — multiple submissions available.

techniques.name: Americana (Keylock)
techniques.goal: Submit opponent with a shoulder lock by pinning their wrist to the mat and rotating their arm in a paint-brush arc motion against the shoulder joint.

initialPosition.body: In full mount. Weight distributed forward, chest heavy and low over opponent. Not sitting upright.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side hand pins their wrist to the mat beside their head. Other hand threads under their arm (between forearm and mat) and grabs your own wrist — figure-four grip.
initialPosition.legs: Grapevine hooks in (toes hooked under their thighs) OR knees squeezed tight to their ribs for stability.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and low — prevents opponent from bridging you off.

greenLights: Opponent pushes on your chest with one or both hands — arm is now extended and accessible
You've pinned their wrist to the mat near their head successfully
Opponent is flat on their back, not actively bridging or shrimping
Opponent's elbow is bent at approximately 90° (americana requires a bent arm)
redLights: Opponent keeps elbows extremely tight to their body — can't isolate the arm
Opponent is actively bridging and shrimping (focus on maintaining mount first!)
Their arm is straight — americana needs a bent arm, switch to armbar instead
You're sitting too upright (easy to be bumped/bridged off)
steps: From mount, pin opponent's wrist to the mat beside their head using your same-side hand. Keep your chest heavy and head low for base.
Slide your other hand UNDER their arm (between forearm and the mat) and grab your own wrist, creating the figure-four/paintbrush grip.
Keep their elbow pinned to the mat using your body weight and chest pressure. Slowly slide their wrist toward their hip in an ARC motion along the mat.
Maintain downward pressure on the elbow while moving the wrist in the arc. The shoulder joint reaches its rotation limit — opponent taps.
complexpieces: If they straighten their arm to defend, immediately transition to a straight armbar
If they roll to their side, switch to a kimura on the exposed arm
Set up with a cross collar choke attempt: when they push your choking hand away, catch the extended arm for the americana
simplifications: Practice the figure-four grip configuration with a partner's arm repeatedly
Drill the wrist-to-hip arc to understand the correct angle of pressure
Focus first on maintaining stable mount pressure before attempting any submission

techniques.name: Ezekiel Choke
techniques.goal: Choke opponent from mount using your own gi sleeve as leverage. One arm behind their head for structure, the other feeds inside your own sleeve to press across their throat.

initialPosition.body: In mount, low and heavy on opponent's chest. Posture is forward, not sitting up.
initialPosition.grips: One arm slides behind opponent's head (under the back of their neck). The free hand reaches INSIDE the sleeve opening of the arm behind the head, knuckles presenting across the front of the throat.
initialPosition.legs: Mount with grapevines or knees tight to their ribs for stability.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and driving forward. Weight keeps opponent flat.

greenLights: You have stable mount with heavy pressure — opponent isn't escaping
Opponent is defending by covering their neck with hands (you can wedge your arm behind their head UNDER their defensive hands)
Opponent is focused on hip escaping, not watching for the choke setup
Your arm is already behind their head — half the work is done
redLights: Opponent is actively bridging and you can't maintain mount (stabilize first)
You can't get your arm behind their head — they're blocking effectively
Your sleeve grip is too shallow to form the choke structure
Opponent has strong frames pushing you away from their upper body
steps: From mount, slide one arm behind opponent's head — under the back of their neck. Settle your weight forward and stay heavy.
With your free hand, reach inside your OWN sleeve opening of the arm behind the head. Your knuckles enter the sleeve from the inside of your forearm.
Present the knife-edge/knuckle side of your free hand across opponent's throat (forearm across the front of the neck).
Squeeze by bringing both elbows TOGETHER and dropping your weight forward. The combination of sleeve pulling and forearm pressure across the throat creates the choke.
complexpieces: Can also be applied from inside closed guard as a surprise (risky but effective)
Set up from a failed americana attempt — your arm is already behind their head from the pin
Apply from half guard top as a surprise attack when opponent is focused on sweeping
simplifications: Practice finding your own sleeve opening quickly — do it blindfolded to build muscle memory
Drill the hand-inside-sleeve mechanic against a cooperative partner or dummy
Focus on the squeeze direction: elbows together and weight forward, not pulling backward

techniques.name: Armbar from Mount
techniques.goal: Hyperextend opponent's elbow from the mounted position by transitioning to S-mount, swinging your leg over their face, and lifting your hips against their arm.

initialPosition.body: High mount with hips near opponent's chest. Transitioning to S-mount (one leg posted up near their head).
initialPosition.grips: Both hands controlling the target arm at the wrist. Other hand on their face, chest, or mat for base during the transition.
initialPosition.legs: S-mount: one knee up near opponent's head (same side as trapped arm), other leg down near their hip for base.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to face toward the trapped arm, shifting weight toward their head for the transition.

greenLights: Opponent pushes against your chest with arms extended — arm is exposed
You've isolated one arm at the wrist successfully
Opponent is flat on their back and not actively bridging
You've achieved high mount or S-mount control
redLights: Opponent keeps both arms very tight and refuses to extend — don't force it
Opponent is actively bucking and bridging (secure mount first)
You don't have wrist control — they'll pull the arm out when you spin
Opponent is turned to their side — they can escape more easily during transition
steps: From high mount, control the target arm at the wrist. Place your other hand on the mat (or their face) for base.
Step your foot up near their head on the SAME SIDE as the trapped arm, entering S-mount. Lean your weight toward their head.
Swing your other leg OVER their face. Sit back while clamping both knees together tightly. Keep their thumb pointing at the ceiling (elbow joint facing you).
Pinch knees tight, control the wrist, lift hips UP and slightly back against their elbow joint for the submission.
complexpieces: If they clasp hands to defend, use a bicep curl, wrist peel, or push their hands toward their feet
Chain: cross collar choke attempt → they push your hand → catch the pushing arm for armbar
If they try to pull the arm out during the spin, transition to triangle
simplifications: Practice the S-mount position hold before adding the armbar (just maintain the position for 30 seconds)
Drill the leg swing-over against no resistance to build the movement pattern
Break into phases: (1) S-mount entry, (2) leg over face, (3) finishing squeeze

techniques.name: Trap & Roll Escape (Upa)
techniques.goal: Bridge opponent over your body to reverse mount position — you end up in their closed guard on top. A power-based escape using explosive hip movement.

initialPosition.body: On your back underneath mount. Arms are defending and about to trap.
initialPosition.grips: Trap opponent's arm on one side: hug their wrist/forearm tight to your chest with an overhook. Other hand can frame on their hip or control their head.
initialPosition.legs: Trap opponent's foot on the SAME side as the trapped arm: step your foot over their ankle, pinning it to the mat.
initialPosition.hips: Loaded and coiled, ready to bridge explosively upward and over.

greenLights: Opponent posts one hand on the mat — you can trap that arm AND foot on the same side
Opponent leans forward with their head over yours (weight shifted forward)
Opponent has both feet flat on the mat — NOT grapevined (no hooks)
Opponent is attempting a choke and momentarily not focused on their base
redLights: Opponent has both arms free and good wide base — nothing is trapped
Opponent is in high mount with grapevine hooks (very hard to bridge)
You've only trapped the arm but NOT the leg — they'll just post their foot to block
Opponent is sitting very upright with wide base on BOTH sides
steps: Trap their arm on one side: HUG their wrist/forearm tight to your chest. Overhook that arm so it can't escape.
Trap their foot on the SAME SIDE: step your foot over their ankle, pinning it to the mat. They now can't post arm or foot on that side.
Bridge EXPLOSIVELY: drive your hips straight UP toward the ceiling, then arc OVER your shoulder toward the trapped side. Imagine punching the ceiling with your hips.
